content: Blockchain replaces the central bank’s role in transaction recording by leveraging a decentralized network of nodes that collectively maintain and validate a distributed ledger. Instead of a single trusted intermediary, consensus protocols—such as proof-of-work or proof-of-stake—ensure agreement on transaction ordering and legitimacy by requiring nodes to solve cryptographic puzzles or stake economic value, thereby aligning incentives and securing the network against Byzantine actors. Transactions are grouped into blocks, each cryptographically linked to its predecessor via a hash, creating an immutable chain that enforces chronological order without a central timestamp authority. Validation occurs through consensus, where nodes verify transactions’ cryptographic signatures and enforce protocol rules before appending blocks. This distributed validation coupled with economic incentives disincentivizes double-spending and censorship, effectively substituting centralized trust with mathematically guaranteed consensus and game-theoretic security. The absence of a central bank shifts settlement finality to probabilistic or eventual consensus, depending on the protocol, fundamentally transforming how trust and transaction integrity are established and maintained.
